summary: In 2017, I decided to return to Guatemala with my children to reconnect with my roots, friends, and family. Visiting Guatemala City and other historical sites like Rio Dulce and Tikal gave me a deep appreciation for my culture. It was a meaningful journey that allowed me to share my heritage with my sons.

One of the things I missed most about Guatemala was the food, especially fiambre. This traditional dish reminded me of home and was a big part of expressing my culture while living in the States. Tasting it again after returning felt like reconnecting with my roots.

I have a deep appreciation for the Maya civilization, especially since nearly 40% of Guatemala's population is indigenous Mayan. Their ancient pottery and art have influenced much of Guatemalan culture, and visiting sites like Tikal brought that history to life for me and my family. The Maya legacy is a vital part of who I am.

During my trip, I visited Rio Dulce with my family and my childhood friend Hugo. We explored the Castle of San Felipe de Lara, a Spanish colonial fort at the entrance to Lake Izabal. It was an unforgettable experience touring this historic site together.
cues: Castle of San Felipe de Lara

summary: Tikal holds a special place in my heart as a beautiful Mayan ruin with five major pyramids built around 700 AD. I have fond memories there with my late uncle, who once caught a monkey named Tammy and gave her to me. Visiting Tikal again with my sons was even more meaningful.

entity: Castle of San Felipe de Lara
summary: The Castle of San Felipe de Lara is a Spanish colonial fort built in the early 17th century to protect the Port of San Antonio. Visiting it with my family and friend Hugo was a highlight of our trip. The fort still stands strong at the entrance of Lake Izabal, and touring it was a memorable experience.
cues: Castle of San Felipe de Lara

summary: Lake Izabal is where the Castle of San Felipe de Lara is located, and it was fascinating to see this area with my family. The lake's entrance was historically important for protecting the Port of San Antonio from English pirates. Visiting this place connected me to Guatemala’s colonial past.
cues: Castle of San Felipe de Lara

summary: The Port of San Antonio was historically guarded by the Castle of San Felipe de Lara to defend against English pirates in the early 1600s. Learning about this during my visit helped me understand the strategic importance of the area. It was interesting to see how history shaped this part of Guatemala.
cues: Castle of San Felipe de Lara

summary: English pirates were a threat to the Port of San Antonio in the early 1600s, which led to the construction of the Castle of San Felipe de Lara. Hearing about their attempts to invade made the history of the fort come alive during my visit. It’s fascinating how these events shaped Guatemala’s colonial defenses.
cues: Castle of San Felipe de Lara

summary: Tammy was a monkey my late uncle caught at Tikal and gave to me as a gift. She was a special part of my childhood memories in Guatemala. Sadly, after I left, Tammy was given to a zoo where she spent the rest of her life in captivity.

Guatemala City, with its 3.7 million people, is the largest city in Central America and was a key destination for me and my sons. We explored many tourist spots there, including historical buildings like the National Palace and the Cathedral. It was important for me to introduce my children to the vibrant culture and history of the capital.

The Guatemalan National Palace, built between 1939 and 1943, houses the Cabinet and the 50th President, Jimmy Cabrera. Visiting this impressive building with my sons was a highlight of our time in Guatemala City. It gave us a glimpse into the political history of the country.

Jimmy Cabrera was the 50th President of Guatemala, and his office is located in the Guatemalan National Palace. Learning about him during our visit helped me share important parts of Guatemalan history with my sons. It was meaningful to connect political history with the places we toured.

The Guatemala City Cathedral is the oldest cathedral in the city, constructed over 81 years from 1782 to 1871. Visiting this historic site with my family was awe-inspiring, and it helped me share the rich religious and architectural heritage of Guatemala with my children. The cathedral’s long construction history fascinated us all.

Volcán de Fuego is a volcano in Guatemala known for its constant low-level activity. During my visit, we saw it from a distance, but shortly after, on June 3rd, 2018, it erupted violently, causing significant damage. Witnessing the volcano reminded me of the powerful natural forces present in my homeland.

The 2018 Volcán de Fuego eruption on June 3rd was a devastating event that caused a lot of damage. I had just visited Guatemala and seen the volcano before it erupted, which made the news especially impactful for me and my family. It was a stark reminder of the natural challenges faced by my country.
